note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence boundaries. Block windows were measured by FFT cross-correlation of each WC-10-01-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono f32 PCM, numpy rfft): the Sigma(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula predicts 270.202s but the ffprobed master is 269.714s (0.488s off, past the +/-0.3s gate). Cross-correlation shows the master is the 15 blocks concatenated with an ~0.4s gap after each; per-file ffprobe durations are inflated ~0.033s each by mp3 encoder-delay padding that the PCM concat strips, and the error accumulates monotonically (diff reaches -0.493s by s15), which is the whole discrepancy. Each block startS is its measured onset, which lands cleanly on the 0.08s mp3-frame grid; final end = ffprobed master duration 269.714s. Spelled acronyms (N-O-C, S-L-A) are kept verbatim from the master script per house caption convention.

phrases.text: This is the Micrologic N-O-C.
One board, watching every customer we support, all at once.
Tech support and customer success both live here.
A tenant's own Command Center covers a single operator;
this one sits above all of them, so you catch trouble before the phone even rings.
Look at the top strip.
A red count of the customers in critical shape right now.
A live badge, and a note that it just refreshed.
That matters: the board updates itself, so you are never staring at stale numbers.
And the search box up here is scoped to this screen, to customers, locations, and incidents.
Under the header, three tabs: all customers, enterprise, general.
All customers is the whole book.
The other two split it by tier, enterprise on one side, general on the other.
It is the same instinct as the location scope you already know.
They aim the board, and they touch nothing underneath.
Tap Enterprise.
Everything tightens to that tier: the counts, the map, the feed.
Tap All Customers and the whole network snaps back.
It is a filter on your view, nothing deeper, and it undoes itself in a single click.
Beside the filters, three small counts.
Critical, warning, healthy.
That is the room in one glance, how many customers sit in each band this minute.
When the critical number climbs, that is your first signal to slow down and look closer,
before you have opened a single account.
Now the saved views.
All Accounts is everything.
Enterprise Critical and Needs Attention
drag the board straight to what deserves your eyes first.
Reach for Needs Attention when you walk in cold and want the short list,
then drop back to All Accounts.
View only, same as the filters.
Across the top, five cards.
Customers, critical, warning, healthy, and average S-L-A.
The first four sort your accounts by health.
The last one is the service level for the whole board,
a single clean number you can quote in a morning stand-up without opening anything else.
Read the small print under each count.
Every card splits enterprise against general.
So a card never just tells you how many.
It tells you which tier is carrying the weight,
and that split is exactly where you decide who gets the first call.
The average S-L-A card earns a second look.
It reads just over ninety-seven percent, with the day-over-day drift right beneath it.
A down arrow is a quiet alarm.
Attainment slipped since yesterday,
and on this board that is worth chasing down before it grows.
Drop to Network Coverage.
Every supported site on one map, painted by health: green, amber, red.
The header counts the customers, the locations, and the states behind them.
This is the shape of the whole network in a single picture,
and it is where a regional pattern jumps out at you.
The map is live under your cursor.
Plus and minus zoom, the arrows go full screen,
and the numbered bubbles group nearby sites by their worst case.
Click a state or a cluster and it drops you into that region, without ever leaving the board.
Swing to the feed on the right.
Three tabs: incidents, locations, customers, each with a live count.
Incidents is the default, and the one you live in during a shift.
The other two re-cut the same trouble by site and by account,
for when you want a different angle on it.
The feed stacks by severity.
Critical up top, then warning, then info and monitoring, each with its own tally.
Hold onto one thing here: these count incidents, while the cards up top count customers.
Two different denominators, so the numbers will not always line up, and that is fine.
Now read one row.
The customer, its tier, the headline of what broke, a tag for the kind of failure,
an owner or a status, and how long it has been running.
That last part matters most.
An old, unassigned critical is the one that should bother you.
Read it here; you work it somewhere else.
So that is the cockpit.
Filters and saved views aim it.
The cards and the map give you the shape at a glance.
The feed tells you what is broken and who has it.
Nothing here changes a thing.
You spot it on this board,
then open the customer, the ticket, or the alert somewhere else to fix it.
Learn this screen well, because on a busy day it is the first thing you open.
